name: Elcmar
description: Elcmar is a figure in Irish mythology, primarily known as the husband of Boann, the goddess of the River Boyne. His role is somewhat enigmatic, often overshadowed by his more prominent wife and her relationship with the Dagda. Elcmar's power is tied to the passage of time and perhaps sovereignty, as the Dagda's affair with Boann resulted in the birth of Aengus, a powerful god associated with youth and love. Some accounts suggest Elcmar was tricked by the Dagda, who used his druidic abilities to make Elcmar believe a single day had passed when in reality nine months had gone by, allowing Aengus to be born without Elcmar suspecting infidelity. This highlights Elcmar's vulnerability to magical deception and the Dagda's cunning. He may have held a position of authority or kingship that made him a target for the Dagda's ambitions. While not a central figure in many tales, Elcmar's story serves as a crucial element in the complex web of relationships and power dynamics within the Tuatha Dé Danann. His story underscores themes of betrayal, deception, and the usurpation of power, and the consequences that can arise when divine beings clash. The limited information available suggests he may have been a local deity or figure of importance in a specific region before being incorporated into the broader Irish mythological narrative. His connection to Boann and the Dagda's machinations makes him a significant, albeit tragic, character in the Irish pantheon.
subtitle: Husband of Boann, tricked by Dagda

keyMyths.title: The Birth of Aengus
keyMyths.description: Elcmar is tricked by the Dagda, who uses magic to conceal his affair with Boann. This deception leads to the birth of Aengus, a powerful god, and highlights the power dynamics within the Tuatha Dé Danann.

keyMyths.title: Boann and the Well
keyMyths.description: Elcmar's wife, Boann, challenges the well of Segais, causing it to overflow and form the River Boyne. This event demonstrates her divine power and independence, indirectly impacting Elcmar's standing.

keyMyths.title: Elcmar's Loss
keyMyths.description: The story of Elcmar primarily focuses on the loss of his wife's honor and the birth of Aengus, highlighting his powerlessness against the Dagda's cunning and magical abilities, emphasizing themes of betrayal and usurpation.

cultural.modernLegacy: Elcmar's modern legacy is subtle, primarily appearing in academic studies of Irish mythology and occasional references in contemporary literature and art that draw upon Celtic themes. He is not a widely recognized figure, but his association with Boann and the Dagda ensures his presence in discussions of the Tuatha Dé Danann and their complex relationships. Some modern pagan and Celtic Reconstructionist groups may acknowledge him, though specific worship practices are rare.

extendedContent.title: Origins and Associations
extendedContent.content: Elcmar's origins are somewhat obscure within Irish mythology. He is primarily known through his marriage to Boann, the goddess of the River Boyne. Some interpretations suggest he may have been a local chieftain or a minor deity associated with the land or time, later integrated into the broader mythological framework. His connection to Bru na Boinne (Newgrange) further links him to ancient burial mounds and potentially to the ancestors of the Tuatha Dé Danann. While not as prominent as other deities, Elcmar’s role as Boann's husband places him within the central narratives of Irish mythology, particularly those concerning the sovereignty of the land and the divine lineage of the gods. His name may derive from Old Irish roots but its etymology is not definitively established, adding to the enigmatic nature of this character. He is a figure of quiet authority, whose power is implied rather than explicitly stated.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths and Stories
extendedContent.content: The primary myth involving Elcmar centers on his relationship with Boann and the Dagda. While Elcmar was away, the Dagda, a prominent god, seduced Boann. This union resulted in the birth of Aengus, a significant figure associated with youth, love, and poetic inspiration. Upon Elcmar's return, the Dagda used his magic to conceal the affair, manipulating time to make it appear as if only a single day had passed, despite Boann being pregnant. This act highlights the Dagda's power and cunning, as well as Elcmar's relative vulnerability or naivete. This story underscores themes of sovereignty, betrayal, and the shifting power dynamics within the Tuatha Dé Danann. Although Elcmar does not actively participate in many other major myths, his presence in this pivotal narrative cements his place in the mythological landscape.

extendedContent.title: Legacy and Influence
extendedContent.content: Elcmar's legacy is intricately tied to the story of Boann and Aengus. While he is not a central figure in many tales, his role as Boann's husband and the cuckolded figure in the Dagda's scheme has lasting implications. The birth of Aengus through this illicit affair has significant consequences for the divine lineage and the power structure within the Tuatha Dé Danann. Elcmar's story serves as a cautionary tale about the vulnerability of even powerful figures to deception and the ever-present themes of sovereignty and betrayal in Irish mythology. His relative obscurity, compared to Boann and the Dagda, also raises questions about the roles of minor deities and the complexities of ancient Irish religious beliefs. His presence, though understated, is a crucial element in understanding the broader narrative of the Tuatha Dé Danann and their interactions.

shortDescription: Elcmar, in Irish mythology, is known as the husband of Boann and is tied to the Dagda's affair and the birth of Aengus. His power relates to time and sovereignty.
